title: Auth Flows Explained
intro: There are several ways to handle authentication — JWTs, sessions, OAuth. Each has trade-offs. Swipe right on correct statements, left on misconceptions.

teach.title: The Big Three Auth Patterns
teach.body: Session-based auth stores a session ID in a cookie and keeps user data on the server. JWT (JSON Web Tokens) encodes user info into a signed token the client stores. OAuth lets users log in with an existing account like Google or GitHub, so you never handle their password at all.

teach.title: JWT vs Session Cookie
teach.code:
Session: Browser sends cookie → Server looks up session in DB → Returns user data

JWT: Browser sends token in header → Server decodes token (no DB lookup) → Returns user data

OAuth: User clicks 'Sign in with Google' → Google verifies identity → Your app gets a token
teach.annotation: Each approach has different trade-offs around scalability, security, and complexity.

teach.title: Three Ways to Prove Your Age
teach.body: Sessions are like showing your ID every time — the bouncer checks a list to confirm you're valid. JWTs are like a stamped wristband — anyone can read the stamp without checking the list, but you can't un-stamp it once it's on. OAuth is like having a friend the bouncer trusts vouch for you — you never have to show your ID at all.

Understanding auth flows means you can offer Google and GitHub login — the signup experience users expect from professional products. Fewer friction points at signup means more users, and more users means more revenue.
For most apps, using an auth provider with OAuth support is the easiest and most secure path. You get Google/GitHub login and don't need to manage passwords yourself.

cards.content: JWTs are stateless — the server doesn't need to store session data in a database to verify them
cards.explanation: Correct. JWTs contain all the info needed to verify the user, encoded and signed. The server just checks the signature without a database lookup.

cards.content: You should store JWTs in localStorage because it's the simplest approach
cards.explanation: localStorage is vulnerable to XSS attacks. Any JavaScript on the page can read it. Prefer httpOnly cookies which JavaScript can't access.

cards.content: OAuth means you never have to store or handle user passwords in your own database
cards.explanation: With OAuth, the identity provider (Google, GitHub) handles passwords. Your app only receives a token confirming the user's identity — much less risky.

cards.content: Once a JWT is issued, there's no way to revoke it before it expires
cards.explanation: This is a real trade-off of JWTs. Since they're stateless, you can't easily invalidate them. Workarounds exist (blocklists, short expiry) but they add complexity.

cards.content: Session-based auth is outdated and should never be used in modern apps
cards.explanation: Sessions are still widely used and work great for server-rendered apps. They're easy to revoke and well-understood. The right choice depends on your architecture.

cards.content: Refresh tokens let you keep sessions alive without making the user log in again every few minutes
cards.explanation: Refresh tokens are long-lived tokens used to get new short-lived access tokens. This balances security (short access windows) with user experience (not constantly re-logging in).
